questions.title: ProteinMPNN Message Passing Update
questions.description: Implement the core message passing step in ProteinMPNN for inverse folding. Given node features and edge features on a k-nearest-neighbor graph, compute updated node embeddings by aggregating neighbor messages.
questions.info: ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al., Science 2022) is a message-passing neural network for fixed-backbone sequence design. It operates on a k-nearest-neighbor graph built from C-alpha distances, where node features represent residue states and edge features encode geometric relationships (distances, orientations). The key innovation is an autoregressive decoding order that avoids information leakage during sequence design. ProteinMPNN achieves ~50% native sequence recovery on monomers and generalizes well to novel backbones. It is widely used as the default inverse folding method in modern protein design pipelines. Reference: Dauparas et al., 'Robust deep learning-based protein sequence design using ProteinMPNN', Science 378, 49-56 (2022).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class MPNNLayer(nn.Module):
    """Single message passing layer for ProteinMPNN-style inverse folding."""
    def __init__(self, node_dim=128, edge_dim=64, hidden_dim=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.message_mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(2 * node_dim + edge_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        )
        self.update_mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(node_dim + hidden_dim, node_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim)
        )

    def forward(self, node_feats, edge_feats, edge_index):
        # node_feats: (N, node_dim), edge_feats: (E, edge_dim)
        # edge_index: (2, E) with [src, dst]
        src, dst = edge_index
        # Compute messages from source to destination
        msg_input = torch.cat([node_feats[src], node_feats[dst], edge_feats], dim=-1)
        messages = {{b1}}
        # Aggregate messages per destination node
        agg = torch.zeros_like(node_feats)
        agg.index_add_(0, {{b2}}, messages)
        # Update node features with residual connection
        updated = self.update_mlp(torch.cat([node_feats, agg], dim=-1))
        return {{b3}}

questions.title: RFdiffusion Forward Noising on Frames
questions.description: Implement the forward noising process for RFdiffusion on SE(3) rigid body frames. Each residue frame is represented by a rotation matrix and a translation vector. Apply Gaussian noise to translations and IGSO(3) noise to rotations.
questions.info: RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) adapts denoising diffusion probabilistic models to protein structure generation by operating on SE(3) residue frames. The forward process adds Gaussian noise to translations and isotropic Gaussian noise on SO(3) (IGSO3) to rotations. The reverse process uses a fine-tuned RoseTTAFold network to predict denoised structures. RFdiffusion excels at unconditional backbone generation, motif scaffolding, and binder design. It achieved experimental success rates of up to 15% for de novo binder design. The model represents each residue as a rigid body frame (rotation + translation), following the SE(3) diffusion framework. Reference: Watson et al., 'De novo design of protein structure and function with RFdiffusion', Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023).

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def random_rotation_perturbation(R, noise_scale):
    """Apply IGSO(3) noise to rotation matrices.
    Sample axis-angle perturbation, convert to rotation matrix, apply."""
    # Sample random axis
    axis = F.normalize(torch.randn(R.shape[0], 3), dim=-1)
    # Sample angle magnitude from Gaussian
    angle = torch.randn(R.shape[0]) * noise_scale
    # Rodrigues formula: R_perturb = I + sin(a)*K + (1-cos(a))*K^2
    K = torch.zeros(R.shape[0], 3, 3)
    K[:, 0, 1] = -axis[:, 2]
    K[:, 0, 2] = axis[:, 1]
    K[:, 1, 0] = axis[:, 2]
    K[:, 1, 2] = -axis[:, 0]
    K[:, 2, 0] = -axis[:, 1]
    K[:, 2, 1] = axis[:, 0]
    eye = torch.eye(3).unsqueeze(0)
    sin_a = angle.unsqueeze(-1).unsqueeze(-1)
    cos_a = angle.unsqueeze(-1).unsqueeze(-1)
    R_perturb = eye + torch.sin(sin_a) * K + (1 - torch.cos(cos_a)) * (K @ K)
    return {{b1}}

def forward_diffusion_step(rotations, translations, t, beta_schedule):
    """Apply one step of forward diffusion to SE(3) frames.
    rotations: (N, 3, 3), translations: (N, 3)"""
    beta_t = beta_schedule[t]
    # Noise translations with Gaussian noise
    trans_noise = torch.randn_like(translations) * torch.sqrt(beta_t)
    noisy_translations = {{b2}}
    # Noise rotations with IGSO(3)
    noisy_rotations = random_rotation_perturbation(rotations, {{b3}})
    return noisy_rotations, noisy_translations

Add Gaussian noise to the translation vectors.
The rotation noise scale is the square root of beta_t, matching the translation noise variance.

questions.title: K-Nearest Neighbor Graph for Protein Backbone
questions.description: Build a k-nearest-neighbor graph from C-alpha atom coordinates, as used in graph-based protein design models like ProteinMPNN.
questions.info: Graph neural networks for protein design operate on sparse graphs constructed from backbone atom positions. ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al., Science 2022) constructs a k-nearest-neighbor graph using C-alpha distances, typically with k=30 neighbors. Edge features encode distances, relative orientations, and dihedral angles between residues. This graph representation captures local structural context efficiently and is preferred over fully-connected attention due to the locality of physical interactions. The k-NN graph is a foundational component shared across many protein GNN architectures including GVP-GNN (Jing et al., ICLR 2021) and ESM-IF (Hsu et al., ICML 2022). Reference: Dauparas et al., Science 378, 49-56 (2022); Jing et al., 'Learning from Protein Structure with Geometric Vector Perceptrons', ICLR 2021.

import torch

def build_knn_graph(ca_coords, k=30):
    """Build k-nearest-neighbor graph from C-alpha coordinates.
    ca_coords: (N, 3) tensor of C-alpha positions.
    Returns edge_index: (2, E) tensor of [src, dst] pairs.
    """
    N = ca_coords.shape[0]
    # Compute pairwise distance matrix
    diff = ca_coords.unsqueeze(0) - ca_coords.unsqueeze(1)  # (N, N, 3)
    dist_matrix = {{b1}}
    # Set self-distances to infinity to exclude self-loops
    dist_matrix.fill_diagonal_(float('inf'))
    # Find k nearest neighbors for each node
    _, knn_indices = {{b2}}
    # Build edge index
    src = torch.arange(N).unsqueeze(1).expand(-1, k).reshape(-1)
    dst = knn_indices.reshape(-1)
    edge_index = {{b3}}
    return edge_index

questions.title: SE(3)-Equivariant Vector Update
questions.description: Implement an SE(3)-equivariant update for geometric vector features on protein residues. Scalar features are invariant; vector features transform equivariantly under rotations.
questions.info: SE(3)-equivariant neural networks maintain proper transformation behavior under rotations and translations, which is critical for 3D molecular modeling. In protein design, models like FrameDiff (Yim et al., ICLR 2024) and Chroma (Ingraham et al., Nature 2023) use SE(3)-equivariant layers to ensure that generated structures are physically valid regardless of coordinate frame. The key principle is that scalar features are rotation-invariant while vector features rotate with the coordinate frame. Geometric Vector Perceptrons (GVP) achieve this by processing scalars with standard MLPs and vectors with learned scalar-gated operations that preserve equivariance. This design ensures the network output transforms consistently with input rotations. Reference: Jing et al., 'Learning from Protein Structure with Geometric Vector Perceptrons', ICLR 2021; Yim et al., 'SE(3) diffusion model with application to protein backbone generation', ICLR 2024.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SE3EquivariantUpdate(nn.Module):
    """GVP-style equivariant update with scalar and vector channels."""
    def __init__(self, scalar_dim=128, vector_dim=16):
        super().__init__()
        self.scalar_dim = scalar_dim
        self.vector_dim = vector_dim
        # MLPs operate only on invariant scalar features
        self.scalar_mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(scalar_dim + vector_dim, scalar_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(scalar_dim, scalar_dim)
        )
        # Linear map for vector channels (no bias to preserve equivariance)
        self.vector_linear = nn.Linear(vector_dim, vector_dim, bias=False)
        self.gate_linear = nn.Linear(scalar_dim, vector_dim)

    def forward(self, scalars, vectors):
        # scalars: (N, scalar_dim), vectors: (N, vector_dim, 3)
        # Compute rotation-invariant features from vectors: norms
        vector_norms = {{b1}}
        # Update scalars using both scalar features and vector norms
        scalar_input = torch.cat([scalars, vector_norms], dim=-1)
        updated_scalars = self.scalar_mlp(scalar_input)
        # Update vectors equivariantly: linear transform + scalar gating
        Wv = self.vector_linear(vectors.transpose(-1, -2)).transpose(-1, -2)
        gates = {{b2}}
        updated_vectors = {{b3}}
        return updated_scalars, updated_vectors

Apply the gate linear layer to updated scalars, then pass through sigmoid to get gating values in [0, 1].
Multiply the transformed vectors by the scalar gates (unsqueeze gates to broadcast over the 3D spatial dimension).

questions.title: Fixed-Backbone Sequence Design Loss
questions.description: Implement the cross-entropy training loss for fixed-backbone sequence design, where the model predicts amino acid identities given a fixed backbone structure.
questions.info: Fixed-backbone sequence design (inverse folding) trains a model to predict amino acid sequences that fold into a given backbone structure. The standard training objective is per-residue cross-entropy loss between predicted logits and native amino acid identities. ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al., Science 2022) uses this objective with teacher forcing during training and autoregressive sampling at inference. ESM-IF (Hsu et al., ICML 2022) uses a similar cross-entropy objective but with a GVP-Transformer encoder. Both models achieve ~50% sequence recovery on native backbones, substantially outperforming earlier Rosetta-based methods. The loss is typically averaged over all residues, with optional masking for unknown or disordered positions. Reference: Dauparas et al., Science 378, 49-56 (2022); Hsu et al., 'Learning inverse folding from millions of predicted structures', ICML 2022.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

def sequence_design_loss(logits, native_seq, mask=None):
    """Cross-entropy loss for fixed-backbone sequence design.
    logits: (B, L, 20) predicted amino acid logits
    native_seq: (B, L) integer labels 0-19
    mask: (B, L) boolean, True for valid residues
    """
    B, L, num_classes = logits.shape
    # Reshape for cross entropy: (B*L, 20) and (B*L,)
    logits_flat = logits.reshape(-1, num_classes)
    labels_flat = native_seq.reshape(-1)
    # Per-residue cross entropy (no reduction)
    per_residue_loss = {{b1}}
    per_residue_loss = per_residue_loss.reshape(B, L)
    if mask is not None:
        # Apply mask and average over valid residues only
        per_residue_loss = {{b2}}
        loss = per_residue_loss.sum() / {{b3}}
    else:
        loss = per_residue_loss.mean()
    return loss

Multiply the per-residue loss by the mask to zero out invalid positions.

questions.title: Motif Scaffolding with Conditional Diffusion
questions.description: Implement the motif scaffolding inference procedure for RFdiffusion: keep motif residue coordinates fixed and denoise only scaffold residues at each diffusion step.
questions.info: Motif scaffolding is a key application of RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) where a functional motif (e.g., an enzyme active site or binding epitope) is held fixed while the model generates a surrounding scaffold structure. During reverse diffusion, motif residue coordinates are replaced with their known values at each denoising step, while scaffold residues are denoised normally. This inpainting-style approach allows conditional generation without retraining. RFdiffusion demonstrated remarkable success in scaffolding challenging motifs, including the RSV site V epitope and influenza hemagglutinin targets, with experimental validation showing high binding affinity. The method supports variable-length scaffolds and multiple disjoint motif segments. Reference: Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023).

import torch

def motif_scaffolding_step(denoiser, noisy_coords, motif_coords,
                          motif_mask, t, noise_schedule):
    """One reverse diffusion step with motif inpainting.
    noisy_coords: (N, 3) current noisy coordinates for all residues
    motif_coords: (N, 3) ground truth coords (only valid where motif_mask=True)
    motif_mask: (N,) boolean mask, True for motif residues
    t: current timestep
    """
    # Replace motif positions with noised version of ground truth at timestep t
    alpha_t = noise_schedule['alpha_bar'][t]
    motif_noise = torch.randn_like(motif_coords) * torch.sqrt(1 - alpha_t)
    noised_motif = {{b1}}
    # Inpaint: set motif residues to noised ground truth
    noisy_coords[motif_mask] = {{b2}}
    # Run denoiser to predict clean coordinates
    predicted_clean = denoiser(noisy_coords, t)
    # After denoising, replace motif positions with true coordinates
    predicted_clean[motif_mask] = {{b3}}
    return predicted_clean

Replace the motif positions in the noisy coordinates with the noised ground truth motif coordinates.
After denoising, replace motif residue positions with the original ground truth motif coordinates.

questions.title: Self-Consistency TM Score (scTM)
questions.description: Implement the self-consistency TM-score (scTM) evaluation pipeline for assessing designability of generated protein backbones.
questions.info: The self-consistency TM-score (scTM) is the primary metric for evaluating designability of computationally generated protein structures. The pipeline is: (1) generate a backbone, (2) design a sequence for that backbone using an inverse folding model like ProteinMPNN, (3) predict the structure of that sequence using a folding model like AlphaFold2 or ESMFold, (4) compute the TM-score between the predicted and designed structure. An scTM > 0.5 indicates the design is 'designable' (the sequence folds back to the intended structure). RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) and Chroma (Ingraham et al., Nature 2023) both use scTM as the key benchmark. Chroma reported >80% designability rates. TM-score ranges from 0 to 1, where 1 indicates perfect structural match. Reference: Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023); Ingraham et al., 'Illuminating protein space with a programmable generative model', Nature 623, 1070-1078 (2023).

import torch
import numpy as np

def tm_score(coords1, coords2):
    """Compute TM-score between two sets of C-alpha coordinates.
    coords1, coords2: (L, 3) numpy arrays.
    """
    L = len(coords1)
    d0 = 1.24 * (L - 15) ** (1.0 / 3.0) - 1.8
    # Compute pairwise distances after optimal superposition (assume pre-aligned)
    distances = np.sqrt(np.sum((coords1 - coords2) ** 2, axis=-1))
    # TM-score formula: (1/L) * sum(1 / (1 + (d_i/d0)^2))
    scores = {{b1}}
    tm = {{b2}}
    return tm

def compute_sctm(generated_backbone, inverse_fold_fn, fold_fn):
    """Full self-consistency pipeline.
    generated_backbone: (L, 3) designed backbone coords
    inverse_fold_fn: backbone -> sequence
    fold_fn: sequence -> predicted coords
    """
    # Step 1: design sequence for the backbone
    designed_sequence = inverse_fold_fn(generated_backbone)
    # Step 2: predict structure from designed sequence
    predicted_coords = fold_fn(designed_sequence)
    # Step 3: compute TM-score between generated and predicted
    sctm = {{b3}}
    return sctm

questions.title: Sequence Recovery Rate for Inverse Folding
questions.description: Compute the amino acid sequence recovery rate, the standard evaluation metric for inverse folding models. Recovery rate measures the fraction of positions where the predicted sequence matches the native sequence.
questions.info: Sequence recovery rate is the standard benchmark metric for inverse folding models. It measures the fraction of residue positions where the designed sequence matches the native (crystallographic) sequence. ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al., Science 2022) achieves ~52% recovery on CATH test sets, compared to ~33% for the earlier StructGNN. ESM-IF (Hsu et al., ICML 2022) achieves ~51% using a GVP-Transformer architecture trained on predicted structures from AlphaFold2. While recovery rate is a useful benchmark, high recovery does not guarantee foldability; conversely, designed sequences with low recovery can still fold correctly since many sequences can adopt the same fold. Per-residue recovery is often stratified by secondary structure type (helix, sheet, loop). Reference: Dauparas et al., Science 378, 49-56 (2022); Hsu et al., ICML 2022.

questions.title: Classifier-Free Guidance for Conditional Protein Generation
questions.description: Implement classifier-free guidance for conditional protein backbone generation with Chroma, where generation is guided toward desired properties (e.g., symmetry, secondary structure content).
questions.info: Chroma (Ingraham et al., Nature 2023) uses classifier-free guidance to enable conditional protein generation without retraining. During training, conditioning information is randomly dropped with some probability, teaching the model both conditional and unconditional distributions. At inference, the guided score combines the unconditional and conditional predictions: score_guided = score_uncond + w * (score_cond - score_uncond), where w is the guidance weight. This allows steering generation toward desired properties such as symmetry groups, secondary structure composition, substructure constraints, and even natural language descriptions via CLIP embeddings. Higher guidance weights produce designs more faithful to the condition but with less diversity. Chroma demonstrated generation of over 2000 unique protein topologies. Reference: Ingraham et al., 'Illuminating protein space with a programmable generative model', Nature 623, 1070-1078 (2023).

import torch

def classifier_free_guidance_step(model, x_t, t, condition,
                                   guidance_weight=2.0):
    """Apply classifier-free guidance for conditional protein generation.
    model: denoising network that takes (x_t, t, condition_or_None)
    x_t: (N, 3) noisy coordinates at timestep t
    condition: conditioning information (e.g., symmetry label)
    guidance_weight: strength of guidance (w)
    """
    # Predict noise with conditioning
    eps_cond = model(x_t, t, condition)
    # Predict noise without conditioning (unconditional)
    eps_uncond = {{b1}}
    # Classifier-free guidance formula
    eps_guided = {{b2}}
    return eps_guided

def guided_reverse_step(model, x_t, t, condition, beta_t, alpha_bar_t,
                        guidance_weight=2.0):
    """Full reverse diffusion step with classifier-free guidance."""
    eps_guided = classifier_free_guidance_step(
        model, x_t, t, condition, guidance_weight
    )
    # DDPM reverse step: x_{t-1} from x_t and predicted noise
    alpha_t = 1 - beta_t
    x_mean = (1 / torch.sqrt(alpha_t)) * (x_t - (beta_t / torch.sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t)) * eps_guided)
    noise = torch.randn_like(x_t) if t > 0 else torch.zeros_like(x_t)
    x_prev = {{b3}}
    return x_prev

Classifier-free guidance: eps_uncond + w * (eps_cond - eps_uncond).

questions.title: Hallucination-Based Protein Design with Confidence Loss
questions.description: Implement the hallucination-based protein design objective that optimizes a sequence to maximize the confidence (pLDDT) of a structure prediction network.
questions.info: Hallucination-based protein design uses differentiable structure prediction models as oracles, optimizing sequences to maximize prediction confidence. The approach, pioneered by Anishchenko et al. (Nature 2021) and developed further in 'constrained hallucination' (Wang et al., 2022), optimizes a continuous relaxation of the sequence (softmax over logits) to maximize predicted pLDDT or minimize predicted error. The intuition is that confident structure predictions correlate with well-folded, designable proteins. This approach generated novel protein folds validated experimentally with high success rates. The Gumbel-softmax trick enables gradient flow through the discrete sequence sampling. Hallucination was a precursor to diffusion-based methods and remains useful for targeted optimization. Reference: Anishchenko et al., 'De novo protein design by deep network hallucination', Nature 600, 547-552 (2021).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

def hallucination_step(structure_predictor, seq_logits, temperature=0.1):
    """One gradient step of hallucination-based protein design.
    structure_predictor: differentiable model that returns
                        (predicted_coords, plddt_per_residue)
    seq_logits: (L, 20) learnable parameters (logits over amino acids)
    temperature: Gumbel-softmax temperature

    soft_seq = {{b1}}
    # Predict structure and confidence from soft sequence
    predicted_coords, plddt = structure_predictor(soft_seq)
    # Loss: negative mean pLDDT (we want to maximize confidence)
    loss = {{b2}}
    return loss, predicted_coords, plddt

def run_hallucination(structure_predictor, seq_length, num_steps=500, lr=0.05):
    """Run full hallucination optimization loop."""
    # Initialize random sequence logits
    seq_logits = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(seq_length, 20))
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([seq_logits], lr=lr)
    for step in range(num_steps):
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss, coords, plddt = hallucination_step(
            structure_predictor, seq_logits
        )
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

    return final_sequence

questions.title: FrameDiff Score Network on SE(3) Frames
questions.description: Implement the score prediction head for FrameDiff, which predicts translation scores and rotation scores from node embeddings of protein residue frames.
questions.info: FrameDiff (Yim et al., ICLR 2024) formulates protein backbone generation as score-based generative modeling on the product manifold SE(3)^N, where each residue is parameterized as an SE(3) frame (rotation + translation). The score network predicts translation scores in R^3 and rotation scores in the tangent space of SO(3) (represented as 3D vectors via the logarithmic map). FrameDiff introduces a novel training objective that decomposes the score into independent translation and rotation components, enabling efficient training. The model uses an Invariant Point Attention (IPA) backbone inspired by AlphaFold2. FrameDiff achieves high designability scores competitive with RFdiffusion on unconditional backbone generation benchmarks. Reference: Yim et al., 'SE(3) diffusion model with application to protein backbone generation', ICLR 2024.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class FrameDiffScoreHead(nn.Module):
    """Score prediction head for SE(3) diffusion on protein frames.
    Predicts translation score (R^3) and rotation score (so(3) tangent vector).
    """
    def __init__(self, node_dim=256):
        super().__init__()
        # Translation score: predict vector in R^3
        self.trans_score_net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(node_dim, 3)
        )
        # Rotation score: predict vector in so(3) tangent space (dim=3)
        self.rot_score_net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(node_dim, 3)
        )

    def forward(self, node_embeddings, frames_R, frames_t):
        """Predict SE(3) scores in the local frame of each residue.
        node_embeddings: (N, node_dim) from IPA backbone
        frames_R: (N, 3, 3) rotation matrices
        frames_t: (N, 3) translation vectors
        """
        # Predict scores in local frame
        local_trans_score = self.trans_score_net(node_embeddings)  # (N, 3)
        local_rot_score = {{b1}}
        # Transform translation score to global frame
        global_trans_score = {{b2}}
        # Rotation scores stay in local tangent space (so(3) is rotation-equivariant)
        return global_trans_score, local_rot_score

def framediff_score_loss(pred_trans_score, pred_rot_score,
                        target_trans_score, target_rot_score):
    """Combined score matching loss for SE(3) diffusion."""
    trans_loss = torch.mean((pred_trans_score - target_trans_score) ** 2)
    rot_loss = torch.mean((pred_rot_score - target_rot_score) ** 2)
    return {{b3}}

questions.title: Protein Graph Edge Features from Geometry
questions.description: Compute geometric edge features for a protein backbone graph, including distances and relative positional encodings between residues, as used in ProteinMPNN and related GNNs.
questions.info: Protein GNN models like ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al., Science 2022) and GVP-GNN (Jing et al., ICLR 2021) encode rich geometric information in edge features. Key features include C-alpha distances, unit direction vectors, relative sequential separation (encoded via sinusoidal positional encoding), and backbone dihedral angles. The distance is typically RBF-expanded into multiple Gaussian basis functions to provide a smooth representation. Direction vectors capture relative orientation and are important for maintaining geometric awareness. These edge features, combined with node features derived from backbone torsion angles and residue type, allow the GNN to reason about 3D structural context for sequence design. Reference: Dauparas et al., Science 378, 49-56 (2022); Jing et al., ICLR 2021.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def rbf_encode(distances, d_min=0.0, d_max=20.0, num_rbf=16):
    """Radial basis function encoding of distances."""
    mu = torch.linspace(d_min, d_max, num_rbf)
    sigma = (d_max - d_min) / num_rbf
    return torch.exp(-((distances.unsqueeze(-1) - mu) ** 2) / (2 * sigma ** 2))

def compute_edge_features(ca_coords, edge_index, max_seq_sep=32):
    """Compute geometric edge features for protein graph.
    ca_coords: (N, 3) C-alpha coordinates
    edge_index: (2, E) source and destination indices
    """
    src, dst = edge_index
    # Distance features (RBF-encoded)

questions.title: Invariant Point Attention (IPA) for Protein Structure
questions.description: Implement a simplified version of Invariant Point Attention from AlphaFold2, which is used in FrameDiff and other SE(3)-aware protein generation models. IPA computes attention using both sequence features and 3D point positions.
questions.info: Invariant Point Attention (IPA) was introduced in AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) and adopted by FrameDiff (Yim et al., ICLR 2024) and other structure generation models. IPA augments standard attention with 3D geometric information by projecting query/key/value points into local residue frames, computing distances in global space, and aggregating results. The key property is SE(3) invariance: the attention output is invariant to global rotations and translations while being sensitive to relative geometry. IPA uses three types of interactions: standard sequence-based attention, pairwise bias from pair representations, and point-based attention computed from 3D distances between projected query and key points. This combination allows the model to reason about both sequence and structural context. Reference: Jumper et al., Nature 596, 583-589 (2021); Yim et al., ICLR 2024.
questions.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

class SimplifiedIPA(nn.Module):
    """Simplified Invariant Point Attention."""
    def __init__(self, node_dim=256, num_heads=8, num_points=4):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = node_dim // num_heads
        self.num_points = num_points
        # Standard QKV projections for sequence features
        self.q_proj = nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim)
        self.k_proj = nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim)
        self.v_proj = nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim)
        # Point projections: each head has num_points query/key points
        self.q_points = nn.Linear(node_dim, num_heads * num_points * 3)
        self.k_points = nn.Linear(node_dim, num_heads * num_points * 3)
        self.output_proj = nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim)

    def forward(self, node_feats, frames_R, frames_t):
        N = node_feats.shape[0]
        h, d, p = self.num_heads, self.head_dim, self.num_points
        # Standard attention logits
        Q = self.q_proj(node_feats).reshape(N, h, d)
        K = self.k_proj(node_feats).reshape(N, h, d)
        V = self.v_proj(node_feats).reshape(N, h, d)
        seq_logits = torch.einsum('ihd,jhd->ijh', Q, K) / math.sqrt(d)
        # Point-based attention: project points to global frame
        q_pts_local = self.q_points(node_feats).reshape(N, h, p, 3)
        k_pts_local = self.k_points(node_feats).reshape(N, h, p, 3)
        # Transform points to global: R @ pt + t
        q_pts_global = torch.einsum('nab,nhpb->nhpa', frames_R, q_pts_local) + frames_t[:, None, None, :]
        k_pts_global = torch.einsum('nab,nhpb->nhpa', frames_R, k_pts_local) + frames_t[:, None, None, :]
        # Point attention: negative squared distance
        pt_diff = q_pts_global[:, None, :, :, :] - k_pts_global[None, :, :, :, :]
        pt_dist_sq = (pt_diff ** 2).sum(dim=-1).sum(dim=-1)  # (N, N, h)
        pt_logits = {{b1}}
        # Combine sequence and point logits
        logits = seq_logits + pt_logits
        weights = {{b2}}
        # Aggregate values
        out = torch.einsum('ijh,jhd->ihd', weights, V)
        out = out.reshape(N, h * d)
        return {{b3}}

questions.title: DDPM Reverse Process for Backbone Generation
questions.description: Implement the DDPM reverse sampling process for protein backbone coordinate generation, as used in diffusion-based protein design.
questions.info: Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) form the backbone of modern protein structure generation methods including RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023), FrameDiff (Yim et al., ICLR 2024), and Genie (Lin & AlQuraishi, NeurIPS 2023). The reverse process iteratively denoises random coordinates to produce valid protein backbones. Starting from pure Gaussian noise, each step uses a trained denoiser to predict the clean structure (or noise), then computes the posterior mean and adds calibrated noise. Genie specifically operates on C-alpha coordinates and uses a SE(3)-invariant transformer. The noise schedule is critical: cosine schedules work better than linear ones for protein structures. Typical generation uses 200-1000 diffusion timesteps. Reference: Lin & AlQuraishi, 'Generating Novel, Designable, and Diverse Protein Structures by Equivariantly Diffusing Oriented Residue Clouds', NeurIPS 2023; Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023).
questions.code:
import torch

def ddpm_reverse_sample(denoiser, num_residues, num_timesteps=200,
                       beta_schedule=None):
    """DDPM reverse sampling for protein backbone coordinates.
    denoiser: model that predicts clean coords from noisy input
    Returns: (num_residues, 3) generated C-alpha coordinates
    """
    if beta_schedule is None:
        beta_schedule = torch.linspace(1e-4, 0.02, num_timesteps)
    alpha = 1.0 - beta_schedule
    alpha_bar = torch.cumprod(alpha, dim=0)
    # Start from pure noise
    x_t = torch.randn(num_residues, 3)
    for t in reversed(range(num_timesteps)):
        # Predict clean structure
        x0_pred = denoiser(x_t, torch.tensor([t]))
        # Compute posterior mean
        alpha_bar_t = alpha_bar[t]
        alpha_t = alpha[t]
        beta_t = beta_schedule[t]
        coeff1 = {{b1}}
        coeff2 = beta_t / torch.sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t)
        posterior_mean = coeff1 * (x_t - coeff2 * (x_t - torch.sqrt(alpha_bar_t) * x0_pred))
        # Add noise (except at t=0)
        if t > 0:
            noise = torch.randn_like(x_t)
            x_t = {{b2}}
        else:
            x_t = {{b3}}
    return x_t

Add scaled noise to the posterior mean: posterior_mean + sqrt(beta_t) * noise.

questions.title: ESM-IF GVP-Transformer Encoder

questions.info: ESM-IF (Hsu et al., ICML 2022) uses a GVP-Transformer architecture for inverse folding, combining Geometric Vector Perceptrons (GVPs) for encoding backbone geometry with transformer layers for sequence-level reasoning. The encoder processes backbone coordinates through GVP layers to produce SE(3)-invariant node features, which are then refined by transformer self-attention layers. ESM-IF was trained on 12 million predicted structures from AlphaFold2, demonstrating that scaling training data with computational predictions improves generalization. It achieves 51% sequence recovery and shows strong performance on zero-shot fitness prediction tasks. The architecture processes N, C-alpha, C, and O backbone atom coordinates to compute local coordinate frames and geometric features. Reference: Hsu et al., 'Learning inverse folding from millions of predicted structures', ICML 2022.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class GVPEncoderLayer(nn.Module):
    """Simplified GVP-Transformer encoder layer for ESM-IF."""
    def __init__(self, scalar_dim=512, vector_dim=16, num_heads=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.scalar_dim = scalar_dim
        # GVP for processing geometric features
        self.gvp_scalar = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(scalar_dim + vector_dim, scalar_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(scalar_dim, scalar_dim)
        )
        self.vector_proj = nn.Linear(vector_dim, vector_dim, bias=False)
        # Transformer attention over scalar features
        self.self_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(scalar_dim, num_heads, batch_first=True)
        self.ffn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(scalar_dim, scalar_dim * 4),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(scalar_dim * 4, scalar_dim)
        )
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(scalar_dim)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(scalar_dim)

    def forward(self, scalars, vectors):
        # scalars: (B, L, scalar_dim), vectors: (B, L, vector_dim, 3)
        # GVP: extract invariant features from vectors
        vec_norms = vectors.norm(dim=-1)  # (B, L, vector_dim)
        gvp_in = torch.cat([scalars, vec_norms], dim=-1)
        scalar_update = self.gvp_scalar(gvp_in)
        scalars = scalars + scalar_update
        # Transformer self-attention on scalar features
        residual = scalars
        scalars = self.norm1(scalars)
        attn_out, _ = {{b1}}
        scalars = residual + attn_out
        # Feed-forward with residual
        residual = scalars
        scalars = self.norm2(scalars)
        ff_out = {{b2}}
        scalars = {{b3}}
        return scalars, vectors

questions.title: Chroma: Graph-Based Diffusion on Protein Residues

Unlike RFdiffusion which fine-tunes a structure prediction network, Chroma trains a GNN-based denoiser from scratch that directly predicts coordinate displacements. Chroma's key innovation is the combination of this graph-based architecture with a rich set of programmatic conditioners, enabling generation conditioned on symmetry, shape, semantics, and natural language. The model generates proteins with up to 4000 residues and achieves >80% designability. Reference: Ingraham et al., 'Illuminating protein space with a programmable generative model', Nature 623, 1070-1078 (2023).

questions.title: Self-Consistency RMSD (scRMSD) Evaluation

questions.info: Self-consistency RMSD (scRMSD) complements scTM as a designability metric. While scTM measures global fold similarity (normalized between 0-1), scRMSD measures the root-mean-square deviation in Angstroms between the designed backbone and the structure predicted from the designed sequence. An scRMSD below 2 Angstroms is generally considered a successful design, indicating the sequence folds to the intended structure with atomic-level accuracy. RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) reports both scTM and scRMSD, with their best unconditional designs achieving scRMSD < 1 Angstrom. FrameDiff (Yim et al., ICLR 2024) and Genie (Lin & AlQuraishi, NeurIPS 2023) also use scRMSD as a primary evaluation metric, often alongside designability rate (fraction with scRMSD < 2A). import torch
import numpy as np

def compute_rmsd(coords1, coords2):
    """Compute RMSD between two coordinate sets after optimal superposition.
    coords1, coords2: (L, 3) numpy arrays
    Assumes coordinates have been centered.
    """
    # Center both structures
    coords1_centered = coords1 - coords1.mean(axis=0)
    coords2_centered = coords2 - coords2.mean(axis=0)
    # Kabsch algorithm: find optimal rotation
    H = coords1_centered.T @ coords2_centered
    U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(H)
    # Handle reflection case
    d = np.sign(np.linalg.det(Vt.T @ U.T))
    diag = np.diag([1, 1, d])
    R_opt = Vt.T @ diag @ U.T
    # Apply rotation and compute RMSD
    coords1_aligned = (R_opt @ coords1_centered.T).T
    diff = coords1_aligned - coords2_centered
    rmsd = {{b1}}
    return rmsd

    """Self-consistency RMSD pipeline."""
    designed_seq = {{b2}}
    predicted_structure = fold_fn(designed_seq)
    scrmsd = {{b3}}
    return scrmsd

questions.title: Cosine Noise Schedule for Protein Diffusion
questions.description: Implement the cosine noise schedule used in protein diffusion models, which provides a smoother noise distribution than linear schedules.
questions.info: The noise schedule is a critical hyperparameter in diffusion models for protein generation. The cosine schedule, introduced by Nichol & Dhariwal (ICML 2021) and widely adopted in protein design, defines alpha_bar_t = cos((t/T + s) / (1 + s) * pi/2)^2, where s is a small offset preventing singularity. RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) uses a modified cosine schedule for translations and a separate schedule for rotations on SO(3). The cosine schedule provides near-linear noise accumulation in the middle timesteps and slower noise addition at the extremes, which better preserves structural features during the early noising steps. Genie (Lin & AlQuraishi, NeurIPS 2023) also uses a cosine schedule and found it superior to linear schedules for protein coordinate diffusion. Reference: Nichol & Dhariwal, ICML 2021; Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023).

import torch
import math

def cosine_noise_schedule(num_timesteps, s=0.008):
    """Cosine noise schedule for diffusion models.
    Returns alpha_bar (cumulative signal retention) for each timestep.
    """
    steps = torch.linspace(0, num_timesteps, num_timesteps + 1)
    # f(t) = cos((t/T + s) / (1 + s) * pi/2)^2
    f_t = {{b1}}
    # alpha_bar_t = f(t) / f(0)
    alpha_bar = f_t / f_t[0]
    # Clip to prevent numerical issues
    alpha_bar = torch.clamp(alpha_bar, min=1e-5, max=1.0)
    # Compute betas from consecutive alpha_bars
    # beta_t = 1 - alpha_bar_t / alpha_bar_{t-1}

    betas = torch.clamp(betas, min=1e-5, max=0.999)
    return {{b3}}

questions.title: Genie: Equivariant Denoiser for Residue Clouds

    def forward(self, node_feats, coords):
        N = coords.shape[0]
        # Build kNN graph from current coords

        knn_dist, knn_idx = dist_mat.topk(self.k, largest=False)

questions.title: Multistate Sequence Design Objective
questions.description: Implement a multistate design loss that optimizes a single sequence to be compatible with multiple target backbone conformations, as used in designing proteins with conformational switches.
questions.info: Multistate protein design optimizes a single amino acid sequence to fold stably into multiple target conformations, enabling the design of conformational switches, allosteric proteins, and fold-switching proteins. The objective jointly minimizes the inverse folding loss across all target states, often with per-state weighting. Hershey et al. (2023) demonstrated multistate design using ProteinMPNN by averaging sequence logits across states. Earlier computational approaches by Davey & Bhatt (PNAS 2017) used Rosetta multistate design with genetic algorithms. The key challenge is balancing stability across states without creating frustrated sequences. Recent diffusion-based methods like multistate RFdiffusion extensions allow simultaneous backbone-sequence co-design for multiple conformations. Reference: Dauparas et al., Science 378, 49-56 (2022); Davey & Bhatt, PNAS 114, E1466-E1475 (2017).

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def multistate_design_loss(model, seq_logits, backbone_states, state_weights=None):
    """Multistate design: optimize one sequence for multiple backbone conformations.
    model: inverse folding model returning logits given backbone
    seq_logits: (L, 20) learnable sequence logits
    backbone_states: list of K backbone feature tensors, each (L, feat_dim)
    state_weights: (K,) optional per-state weights, uniform if None
    """
    K = len(backbone_states)
    if state_weights is None:
        state_weights = torch.ones(K) / K
    # Soft sequence via Gumbel-softmax
    soft_seq = F.gumbel_softmax(seq_logits, tau=0.1, hard=False)
    total_loss = 0.0
    for i, backbone in enumerate(backbone_states):
        # Get predicted logits for this backbone state
        pred_logits = model(backbone)  # (L, 20)
        # Cross-entropy between predicted distribution and soft target
        log_probs = {{b1}}
        per_residue_loss = -(soft_seq * log_probs).sum(dim=-1)  # (L,)
        state_loss = per_residue_loss.mean()
        total_loss = total_loss + {{b2}}
    return {{b3}}

questions.title: Symmetric Protein Design with Tied Sequences

This is achieved by averaging logits across symmetry-related positions before sampling.     backbone_feats: (N_total, feat_dim) features for all chains concatenated
    symmetry_groups: list of lists, each inner list has indices of
                     symmetry-equivalent residues across chains
                     e.g., [[0,100,200], [1,101,201], ...] for a trimer
    Returns: (N_total,) designed sequence with symmetry enforced
    """

questions.title: Antibody CDR Loop Generation

questions.info: Antibody design focuses on generating complementarity-determining region (CDR) loops, particularly the highly variable CDR-H3 loop, which is the primary determinant of antigen binding specificity. DiffAb (Luo et al., ICLR 2023) uses an equivariant diffusion model to jointly generate CDR loop sequences, structures, and orientations conditioned on the antibody framework. The model operates on residue-level features and uses SE(3)-equivariant attention to maintain geometric consistency. AbDiffuser (Martinkus et al., NeurIPS 2023) extends full-atom diffusion to antibody CDR design with side-chain generation. import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class CDRLoopGenerator(nn.Module):
    """Diffusion-based CDR loop generator conditioned on framework."""
    def __init__(self, node_dim=256, num_layers=6):
        super().__init__()
        self.framework_encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim)

        self.cdr_denoiser = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(node_dim + 3 + 1, node_dim),  # +3 for coords, +1 for timestep
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(node_dim, 3 + 20)  # predict coords (3) + seq logits (20)
        )
        self.cross_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(node_dim, 8, batch_first=True)

        framework_feats: (L_fw, node_dim) framework residue features
        cdr_noisy_coords: (L_cdr, 3) noisy CDR coordinates
        cdr_mask: (L_total,) boolean mask indicating CDR positions
        t: scalar timestep

        seq_logits = {{b3}}

The loss balances geometric satisfaction with overall backbone quality. Recent work by Wang et al. (2024) demonstrated enzyme design with RFdiffusion achieving catalytic activities rivaling natural enzymes. Reference: Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023); Rothlisberger et al., Nature 453, 190-195 (2008).

def enzyme_design_loss(pred_coords, catalytic_residues, target_distances,

questions.title: Protein-Protein Interface Energy Scoring

questions.info: Protein-protein interface design requires scoring the energetic favorability of designed interfaces. The binding energy (ddG) combines van der Waals interactions, electrostatics, hydrogen bonding, and desolvation penalties. Rosetta's interface energy function (Leaver-Fay et al., Methods in Enzymology 2011) uses a Lennard-Jones potential for van der Waals and a Coulombic term with distance-dependent dielectric for electrostatics. Modern differentiable scoring functions like AttnPacker (McPartlon & Bhatt, ICLR 2023) learn these energy terms as neural network potentials. The shape complementarity at the interface is quantified by the Lawrence & Colman Sc metric (1993), which measures geometric surface matching. Interface-designed proteins from RFdiffusion show high shape complementarity (Sc > 0.6) and favorable computed binding energies. Reference: Leaver-Fay et al., Methods in Enzymology 487, 545-574 (2011); Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023).

def interface_energy_score(binder_atoms, target_atoms,

                          vdw_radii_b, vdw_radii_t,

    vdw_radii_b, vdw_radii_t: (N_b,), (N_t,) van der Waals radii

    total_energy = {{b3}}
    return total_energy

        sse2_center = coords[topology_spec[k+1]['start']:topology_spec[k+1]['end']].mean(dim=0)

AlphaFold3 (Abramson et al., Nature 2024) uses a diffusion module that operates on all heavy atoms, predicting denoised atomic coordinates from noisy inputs. import torch

def all_atom_forward_diffusion(atom_coords, atom_mask, alpha_bar_t,
                               noise_scale_backbone=1.0, noise_scale_sidechain=1.0,
                               backbone_atom_mask=None):

    atom_coords: (N_res, max_atoms, 3) atom positions per residue
    atom_mask: (N_res, max_atoms) boolean, True for existing atoms
    alpha_bar_t: scalar noise level
    backbone_atom_mask: (N_res, max_atoms) True for backbone atoms (N, CA, C, O)
    """
    noise = torch.randn_like(atom_coords)
    # Different noise scales for backbone and sidechain atoms
    noise_scales = torch.ones_like(atom_coords[:, :, 0:1])
    if backbone_atom_mask is not None:
        noise_scales[backbone_atom_mask] = noise_scale_backbone
        noise_scales[~backbone_atom_mask] = noise_scale_sidechain
    scaled_noise = noise * noise_scales
    # Forward diffusion: x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar) * x_0 + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar) * noise
    signal_rate = {{b1}}
    noise_rate = torch.sqrt(1.0 - alpha_bar_t)
    noisy_coords = signal_rate * atom_coords + noise_rate * scaled_noise
    # Zero out positions for non-existent atoms
    noisy_coords = {{b2}}
    return noisy_coords, scaled_noise

    """Per-atom MSE loss for all-atom diffusion, masked for valid atoms."""
    diff = (predicted_coords - true_coords) ** 2
    per_atom_mse = diff.sum(dim=-1)  # sum over xyz -> (N_res, max_atoms)
    masked_mse = per_atom_mse * atom_mask.float()
    loss = {{b3}}
    return loss

questions.title: Side Chain Packing with Rotamer Sampling
questions.description: Implement a neural network-based side chain packing module that predicts chi dihedral angles for each residue given the backbone structure and sequence.
questions.info: Side chain packing determines the 3D positions of amino acid side chain atoms given a fixed backbone and sequence. Classical methods like Rosetta's Packer (Leaver-Fay et al., Methods in Enzymology 2011) use rotamer libraries and Monte Carlo optimization. AttnPacker (McPartlon & Bhatt, ICLR 2023) uses a neural network to directly predict chi angles, achieving accuracy competitive with physics-based methods at much faster speeds. DLPacker (Misiura et al., 2022) uses 3D convolutions on density maps. Side chains are parameterized by up to 4 chi dihedral angles depending on residue type. The prediction is typically decomposed into per-residue chi angle predictions, conditioned on backbone geometry and neighboring residue identities. Accurate side chain packing is essential for evaluating designed protein stability and computing binding energies. Reference: McPartlon & Bhatt, 'An end-to-end equivariant model for protein side-chain packing', ICLR 2023; Leaver-Fay et al., Methods in Enzymology 487, 545-574 (2011).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class SideChainPacker(nn.Module):
    """Neural side chain packing: predict chi angles from backbone + sequence."""
    def __init__(self, node_dim=256, num_chi_bins=36):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_chi_bins = num_chi_bins  # discretize each chi into 36 bins (10 deg each)
        self.seq_embed = nn.Embedding(20, node_dim)
        self.backbone_encoder = nn.Linear(9, node_dim)  # N, CA, C coords flattened
        self.predictor = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(node_dim * 2, node_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(node_dim, node_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(node_dim, 4 * num_chi_bins)  # 4 chi angles, each with num_bins logits
        )

    def forward(self, backbone_coords, seq_indices, chi_mask):
        """Predict chi angles for side chain packing.
        backbone_coords: (N, 3, 3) N, CA, C atom coords per residue
        seq_indices: (N,) amino acid indices 0-19
        chi_mask: (N, 4) boolean, which chi angles exist for each residue
        """
        N = seq_indices.shape[0]
        seq_feats = {{b1}}
        bb_feats = self.backbone_encoder(backbone_coords.reshape(N, -1))  # (N, node_dim)
        combined = torch.cat([seq_feats, bb_feats], dim=-1)  # (N, 2*node_dim)
        logits = self.predictor(combined)  # (N, 4 * num_chi_bins)
        logits = logits.reshape(N, 4, self.num_chi_bins)  # (N, 4, num_bins)
        # Convert bin predictions to angles (bin centers)
        bin_centers = torch.linspace(-180, 180, self.num_chi_bins + 1)[:-1] + 5.0  # degrees
        probs = {{b2}}
        pred_chi = (probs * bin_centers.unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0)).sum(dim=-1)  # (N, 4)
        # Mask out non-existent chi angles
        pred_chi = {{b3}}
        return pred_chi, logits

questions.title: Differentiable Rosetta-Style Energy Function
questions.description: Implement key terms of a differentiable Rosetta-style energy function for protein design, including van der Waals, backbone hydrogen bonding, and solvation terms.
questions.info: The Rosetta energy function (REF2015) is the standard physics-based scoring function for protein design, combining van der Waals interactions (fa_atr, fa_rep), backbone hydrogen bonds (hbond_sr_bb, hbond_lr_bb), solvation (fa_sol), electrostatics (fa_elec), and reference energies (ref). Leaver-Fay et al. (Methods in Enzymology 2011) describes the architecture of the scoring framework. The van der Waals term uses a modified Lennard-Jones potential with linear repulsion below a distance threshold. Hydrogen bond scoring uses an orientation-dependent function considering donor-acceptor distance, angles, and planarity. Making these terms differentiable enables gradient-based sequence optimization as in MCMC-free design. TERMinator (Li et al., NeurIPS 2023) learned differentiable approximations of Rosetta energy terms using neural networks. Reference: Leaver-Fay et al., Methods in Enzymology 487, 545-574 (2011); Park et al., J. Chem. Theory Comput. 12, 6201-6212 (2016).

import torch

def rosetta_vdw_term(atom_coords, atom_radii, well_depth, clash_threshold=0.6):
    """Differentiable van der Waals energy (fa_atr + fa_rep).
    atom_coords: (M, 3) all atom coordinates
    atom_radii: (M,) van der Waals radii
    well_depth: (M,) LJ well depth per atom
    """
    dist = torch.cdist(atom_coords, atom_coords)  # (M, M)
    dist = torch.clamp(dist, min=0.01)
    sigma = atom_radii.unsqueeze(0) + atom_radii.unsqueeze(1)  # (M, M)
    eps = torch.sqrt(well_depth.unsqueeze(0) * well_depth.unsqueeze(1))
    ratio = sigma / dist
    # Attractive term (standard LJ attractive)
    fa_atr = {{b1}}
    # Repulsive: linear ramp below clash threshold
    clash_dist = clash_threshold * sigma
    fa_rep = torch.where(
        dist < clash_dist,
        10.0 * (clash_dist - dist) / clash_dist,
        torch.zeros_like(dist)
    )
    # Mask self-interactions (diagonal)
    mask = 1.0 - torch.eye(atom_coords.shape[0])
    return (mask * (fa_atr + fa_rep)).sum() / 2

def hbond_energy(donor_coords, acceptor_coords, donor_H_coords, ideal_dist=2.0):
    """Backbone hydrogen bond energy."""
    d_ha = torch.norm(donor_H_coords - acceptor_coords, dim=-1)  # H-A distance
    d_da = torch.norm(donor_coords - acceptor_coords, dim=-1)  # D-A distance
    # Distance-dependent score
    dist_score = {{b2}}
    # Angle-dependent: D-H...A angle should be near 180 degrees
    v_dh = donor_H_coords - donor_coords
    v_ha = acceptor_coords - donor_H_coords
    cos_angle = {{b3}}
    angle_score = (cos_angle + 1.0) / 2.0  # 1 when linear, 0 when bent
    return -(dist_score * angle_score).sum()

Use a Gaussian centered at the ideal H-bond distance to score the hydrogen-acceptor distance.
Compute the cosine of the angle between D-H and H-A vectors using cosine similarity.

questions.title: MCMC Sequence Optimization with Metropolis-Hastings
questions.description: Implement Metropolis-Hastings MCMC sequence optimization for protein design, where single amino acid mutations are proposed and accepted based on an energy function.
questions.info: Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sequence optimization is a classical approach to protein design used extensively in Rosetta (Kuhlman et al., Science 2003). The Metropolis-Hastings algorithm proposes random single-residue mutations and accepts or rejects them based on the Boltzmann criterion: accept if the energy decreases, or with probability exp(-dE/kT) if it increases. Simulated annealing improves convergence by gradually decreasing the temperature. Modern hybrid approaches combine MCMC with neural network energy functions: for example, using ProteinMPNN pseudo-log-likelihoods as the scoring function instead of Rosetta energies. Norn et al. (PNAS 2021) used MCMC with trRosetta-predicted distance maps as the objective, achieving hallucination of novel protein structures. The temperature schedule and mutation strategy strongly influence convergence and diversity. Reference: Kuhlman et al., Science 302, 1364-1368 (2003); Norn et al., PNAS 118, e2017228118 (2021).

import torch
import random
import math

def mcmc_sequence_design(energy_fn, backbone, initial_seq, num_steps=10000,
                        T_init=2.0, T_final=0.1):
    """MCMC sequence optimization with simulated annealing.
    energy_fn: function(backbone, sequence) -> scalar energy
    backbone: fixed backbone features
    initial_seq: (L,) starting amino acid indices
    """
    L = initial_seq.shape[0]
    current_seq = initial_seq.clone()
    current_energy = energy_fn(backbone, current_seq)
    best_seq = current_seq.clone()
    best_energy = current_energy
    for step in range(num_steps):
        # Simulated annealing temperature
        T = T_init * (T_final / T_init) ** (step / num_steps)
        # Propose mutation: random position, random amino acid
        pos = random.randint(0, L - 1)
        new_aa = random.randint(0, 19)
        proposed_seq = current_seq.clone()
        proposed_seq[pos] = new_aa
        # Evaluate proposed sequence
        proposed_energy = energy_fn(backbone, proposed_seq)
        delta_E = {{b1}}
        # Metropolis criterion
        if delta_E < 0 or random.random() < {{b2}}:
            current_seq = proposed_seq
            current_energy = proposed_energy
            if current_energy < best_energy:
                best_seq = current_seq.clone()
                best_energy = {{b3}}
    return best_seq, best_energy

The Boltzmann acceptance probability is exp(-delta_E / T).
Update the best energy to the current energy value.

questions.title: Gradient-Based Continuous Sequence Optimization
questions.description: Implement gradient-based sequence optimization using a continuous relaxation of the discrete amino acid sequence, with straight-through estimator for gradient flow.
questions.info: Gradient-based sequence optimization uses backpropagation through differentiable structure prediction or energy models to optimize sequences. Unlike MCMC, which explores discrete sequence space, gradient methods operate on a continuous relaxation (softmax logits over amino acids). The straight-through Gumbel-softmax estimator (Jang et al., ICLR 2017) enables gradient flow through discrete sampling. Moffat et al. (Cell Systems 2024) demonstrated gradient-based design using ESMFold as a differentiable oracle. SPIN-DIFFUSION (Alamdari et al., 2023) uses gradient-guided diffusion for sequence optimization. Key tricks include entropy regularization to prevent premature collapse to a single amino acid and diversity-promoting losses. The continuous relaxation allows joint optimization of multiple objectives (stability, binding, solubility) with gradient descent. Reference: Jang et al., 'Categorical Reparameterization with Gumbel-Softmax', ICLR 2017; Moffat et al., Cell Systems 15, 2024.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

def gradient_sequence_optimization(oracle, backbone, seq_length,
                                   num_steps=300, lr=0.1,
                                   entropy_weight=0.01):
    """Optimize sequence via gradient descent on continuous relaxation.
    oracle: differentiable function(backbone, soft_seq) -> energy (lower = better)
    backbone: fixed backbone features
    """
    # Initialize learnable logits
    logits = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(seq_length, 20))
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam([logits], lr=lr)
    for step in range(num_steps):
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        # Temperature annealing: start soft, end hard
        tau = max(0.1, 1.0 - step / num_steps)
        # Straight-through Gumbel-softmax
        soft_seq = F.gumbel_softmax(logits, tau=tau, hard=True)
        # Compute energy
        energy = oracle(backbone, soft_seq)
        # Entropy regularization to maintain diversity
        probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
        entropy = {{b1}}
        # Total loss: energy minus entropy bonus
        loss = {{b2}}
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
    # Final discrete sequence
    final_seq = {{b3}}
    return final_seq

Subtract the weighted entropy from the energy to encourage diversity.
Take the argmax over amino acid logits to obtain the final discrete sequence.

questions.title: Diversity Metrics for Generated Protein Ensembles
questions.description: Implement diversity metrics for evaluating a set of generated protein structures, including pairwise TM-score diversity, sequence diversity, and cluster count.
questions.info: Evaluating diversity is critical for protein generative models: a model that generates diverse, novel proteins is more useful than one producing near-identical outputs. Key metrics include: (1) pairwise TM-score, where lower average pairwise TM-score indicates greater structural diversity; (2) sequence identity, measured as the fraction of identical residues between aligned sequences; (3) cluster count, the number of structural clusters at a given TM-score threshold. FoldSeek (van Kempen et al., Nature Biotechnology 2024) provides fast structural clustering for large-scale diversity analysis. Diversity-designability tradeoffs are common: higher sampling temperatures increase diversity but may decrease designability. Reference: Ingraham et al., Nature 623, 1070-1078 (2023); Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023).

questions.title: Novelty Metrics: Max TM-Score to Training Set
questions.description: Implement novelty metrics that measure how different generated protein structures are from known structures in the PDB, using maximum TM-score to the training set.
questions.info: Novelty evaluation determines whether generated proteins represent genuinely new folds rather than memorized training examples. The standard metric is max-TM: for each generated structure, compute its TM-score against all structures in the training set (or PDB) and report the maximum. A max-TM below 0.5 indicates a novel fold not present in the training data. FoldSeek (van Kempen et al., Nature Biotechnology 2024) enables fast structural search against millions of PDB entries. RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) demonstrated generation of structures with max-TM < 0.5 to the PDB, representing genuinely novel folds. import torch
import numpy as np

def compute_max_tm(generated_coords, training_set_coords, tm_score_fn):
    """Compute max TM-score of a generated structure against a training set.
    generated_coords: (L_gen, 3) coordinates of generated structure
    training_set_coords: list of (L_i, 3) coordinate arrays from training set
    tm_score_fn: function(coords1, coords2) -> TM-score
    Returns: max TM-score (float), index of most similar training structure

    max_tm = 0.0
    max_idx = -1
    for idx, train_coords in enumerate(training_set_coords):
        tm = tm_score_fn(generated_coords, train_coords)
        if tm > max_tm:
            max_tm = tm
            max_idx = idx
    return max_tm, max_idx

                  novelty_threshold=0.5):

    Returns: fraction novel, per-structure max-TM scores

    max_tms = []
    for gen_coords in generated_structures:
        max_tm, _ = compute_max_tm(gen_coords, training_set, tm_score_fn)
        max_tms.append(max_tm)
    max_tms = {{b1}}
    # Fraction of generated structures that are novel (below threshold)
    novel_mask = {{b2}}
    fraction_novel = {{b3}}
    return fraction_novel, max_tms

questions.title: Ramachandran Plot Validation for Generated Backbones
questions.description: Implement Ramachandran plot analysis for validating generated protein backbones by computing phi/psi dihedral angles and checking whether they fall in allowed regions.
questions.info: The Ramachandran plot (Ramachandran et al., J. Mol. Biol. 1963) maps backbone dihedral angles phi and psi for each residue, and is a fundamental validation tool for protein structures. Allowed regions in the plot correspond to sterically feasible backbone conformations: alpha-helix (phi~-60, psi~-45), beta-sheet (phi~-120, psi~130), and left-handed helix (phi~60, psi~45). Generated protein structures should have >90% of residues in favored regions and <1% in outlier regions, as assessed by tools like MolProbity (Williams et al., Protein Science 2018). Diffusion-generated backbones from RFdiffusion and Chroma consistently achieve >95% Ramachandran-favored residues, demonstrating that these models learn valid backbone geometry. Glycine and proline have distinct allowed regions due to their unique side chain properties. Reference: Ramachandran et al., J. Mol. Biol. 7, 95-99 (1963); Williams et al., Protein Science 27, 293-315 (2018).

import torch
import math

def compute_dihedral(p0, p1, p2, p3):
    """Compute dihedral angle between four points.
    Returns angle in degrees.
    """
    b1 = p1 - p0
    b2 = p2 - p1
    b3 = p3 - p2
    n1 = torch.cross(b1, b2)
    n2 = torch.cross(b2, b3)
    n1 = n1 / (n1.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-8)
    n2 = n2 / (n2.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-8)
    cos_angle = (n1 * n2).sum(dim=-1).clamp(-1, 1)
    sin_angle = (torch.cross(n1, n2) * b2 / (b2.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-8)).sum(dim=-1)
    return torch.atan2(sin_angle, cos_angle) * 180.0 / math.pi

def compute_phi_psi(N_coords, CA_coords, C_coords):
    """Compute phi and psi angles for a protein backbone.
    N_coords, CA_coords, C_coords: each (L, 3)
    Returns: phi (L-2,), psi (L-2,) in degrees
    """
    # Phi: C(i-1) - N(i) - CA(i) - C(i)
    phi = {{b1}}
    # Psi: N(i) - CA(i) - C(i) - N(i+1)
    psi = {{b2}}
    # Trim to matching length
    min_len = min(len(phi), len(psi))
    return phi[:min_len], psi[:min_len]

def ramachandran_favored_fraction(phi, psi):
    """Fraction of residues in Ramachandran favored regions."""
    # Simplified favored regions: alpha-helix and beta-sheet
    alpha_helix = (phi > -100) & (phi < -30) & (psi > -67) & (psi < -7)
    beta_sheet = (phi > -160) & (phi < -60) & (psi > 90) & (psi < 180)
    favored = {{b3}}
    return favored.float().mean()

Psi is the dihedral defined by N, CA, C of the current residue and N of the next residue.
A residue is in a favored region if it falls in either the alpha-helix or beta-sheet region.

questions.title: Steric Clash Score for Structure Validation

questions.info: The clash score counts the number of serious steric overlaps per 1000 atoms, where an overlap is defined as two non-bonded atoms closer than the sum of their van der Waals radii minus a tolerance (typically 0.4 Angstroms). MolProbity (Williams et al., Protein Science 2018) defines clashes as atom pairs with overlap >= 0.4A. A good structure should have a clash score below 10 (clashes per 1000 atoms). RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) outputs are typically refined with Rosetta FastRelax to resolve clashes. The clash score is complementary to the Ramachandran analysis: together they assess both backbone validity and packing quality. Higher clash scores indicate physically unrealizable structures that would require further optimization. Reference: Williams et al., Protein Science 27, 293-315 (2018); Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023).

questions.title: pLDDT-Based Confidence Filtering for Designs
questions.description: Implement a confidence-based filtering pipeline for generated protein designs using predicted local distance difference test (pLDDT) scores from structure prediction models.
questions.info: The predicted local distance difference test (pLDDT) is a per-residue confidence score output by AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) and ESMFold (Lin et al., Science 2023), ranging from 0-100. Higher pLDDT indicates greater confidence that the predicted structure is correct at that position. In protein design workflows, pLDDT filtering removes low-confidence designs: a design is typically accepted if its mean pLDDT exceeds 70-80 and if less than 10% of residues have pLDDT below 50. Watson et al. (Nature 2023) used pLDDT > 80 as a quality filter for RFdiffusion outputs. pLDDT correlates with structural accuracy and local packing quality. Low pLDDT regions often correspond to disordered loops, poorly packed cores, or regions with backbone strain. Combined with scTM filtering, pLDDT provides a comprehensive quality assessment pipeline. import torch

def plddt_filter(plddt_scores, mean_threshold=80.0, min_threshold=50.0,
                 max_low_fraction=0.1):

    plddt_scores: (L,) per-residue pLDDT scores (0-100)
    Returns: (passed: bool, stats: dict)

    low_confidence = plddt_scores < min_threshold
    fraction_low = {{b2}}
    passed = (mean_plddt >= mean_threshold) and (fraction_low <= max_low_fraction)
    stats = {
        'mean_plddt': mean_plddt.item(),
        'min_plddt': plddt_scores.min().item(),
        'max_plddt': plddt_scores.max().item(),
        'fraction_low': fraction_low.item(),
        'passed': passed

    return passed, stats

    """Filter a batch of designs, returning only those passing pLDDT filter.
    designs: list of design dicts with 'sequence' and 'backbone' keys
    plddt_fn: function(sequence) -> pLDDT scores

    for design in designs:
        plddt = plddt_fn(design['sequence'])
        passed, stats = plddt_filter(plddt, mean_threshold=mean_threshold)
        if passed:
            design['plddt_stats'] = stats
            passed_designs.append(design)

questions.title: Backbone Geometry Featurizer for Protein Design
questions.description: Implement a backbone geometry featurizer that computes torsion angles (phi, psi, omega) and inter-residue orientations from N, CA, C atom coordinates for use in protein design GNNs.
questions.info: Backbone geometry features are the primary input to structure-based protein design models. ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al., Science 2022) uses backbone torsion angles (phi, psi, omega), inter-residue distances, and unit direction vectors between backbone atoms. The local coordinate frame at each residue is defined by the N-CA-C triangle, enabling computation of rotation-invariant relative orientations. GVP-GNN (Jing et al., ICLR 2021) additionally uses virtual C-beta positions computed from backbone geometry. The inter-residue orientation features include the quaternion or Euler angle representation of the relative rotation between local frames, which captures the 3D spatial relationship between residues beyond simple distance. These features are critical for the model to understand secondary structure, backbone curvature, and inter-residue packing geometry. Reference: Dauparas et al., Science 378, 49-56 (2022); Jing et al., ICLR 2021.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def compute_local_frames(N_coords, CA_coords, C_coords):
    """Compute local coordinate frame at each residue from backbone atoms.
    Returns: rotation matrices (L, 3, 3) and origins (L, 3)
    """
    # X-axis: CA -> C direction
    x_axis = F.normalize(C_coords - CA_coords, dim=-1)
    # Temporary vector: CA -> N
    v = N_coords - CA_coords
    # Z-axis: perpendicular to x_axis and v
    z_axis = {{b1}}
    z_axis = F.normalize(z_axis, dim=-1)
    # Y-axis: completes right-handed frame
    y_axis = torch.cross(z_axis, x_axis, dim=-1)
    # Stack into rotation matrix (L, 3, 3)
    R = torch.stack([x_axis, y_axis, z_axis], dim=-1)  # columns are axes
    return R, CA_coords  # origin at CA

def compute_relative_orientation(R_i, t_i, R_j, t_j):
    """Compute relative rotation and translation between two frames.
    R_i, R_j: (3, 3) rotation matrices
    t_i, t_j: (3,) translation vectors
    Returns: relative rotation (3, 3), relative translation (3,)
    """
    # Express frame j in the coordinate system of frame i
    R_rel = {{b2}}
    t_rel = R_i.T @ (t_j - t_i)
    return R_rel, t_rel

def featurize_backbone(N_coords, CA_coords, C_coords, edge_index):
    """Compute geometric features for each edge in the protein graph."""
    R, origins = compute_local_frames(N_coords, CA_coords, C_coords)
    src, dst = edge_index
    features_list = []
    for s, d in zip(src, dst):
        R_rel, t_rel = compute_relative_orientation(R[s], origins[s], R[d], origins[d])
        # Features: relative translation (3) + flattened relative rotation (9)
        feat = {{b3}}
        features_list.append(feat)
    return torch.stack(features_list)

questions.title: Pair Representation Update for Structure Generation
questions.description: Implement the pair representation update with outer product mean and triangular attention, as used in AlphaFold2-derived structure generation models.
questions.info: The pair representation in AlphaFold2 (Jumper et al., Nature 2021) captures pairwise relationships between all residue pairs and is updated using outer product mean from MSA features and triangular multiplicative updates. Structure generation models like FrameDiff (Yim et al., ICLR 2024) and RF2-based methods inherit this pair representation architecture. The outer product mean computes pair features by taking the outer product of single-representation rows, capturing co-evolutionary signals. Triangular attention enforces consistency: if residue i is close to j, and j to k, then the i-k pair should reflect this transitivity. The pair representation directly informs the IPA attention mechanism and is critical for long-range contact prediction. These updates are computationally expensive (O(L^2) memory) but essential for accurate structure modeling. Reference: Jumper et al., Nature 596, 583-589 (2021); Yim et al., ICLR 2024.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class PairRepresentationUpdate(nn.Module):
    """Update pair representation with outer product mean and triangle attention."""
    def __init__(self, node_dim=256, pair_dim=128, hidden_dim=32):
        super().__init__()
        # Outer product mean projections
        self.left_proj = nn.Linear(node_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.right_proj = nn.Linear(node_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.opm_output = nn.Linear(hidden_dim * hidden_dim, pair_dim)
        # Triangular multiplicative update (outgoing)
        self.tri_left = nn.Linear(pair_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.tri_right = nn.Linear(pair_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.tri_gate_left = nn.Linear(pair_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.tri_gate_right = nn.Linear(pair_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.tri_output = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, pair_dim)
        self.layer_norm = nn.LayerNorm(pair_dim)

    def outer_product_mean(self, node_feats):
        """Compute pair features via outer product of node features.
        node_feats: (L, node_dim)
        Returns: (L, L, pair_dim)
        """
        L = node_feats.shape[0]
        left = self.left_proj(node_feats)   # (L, hidden)
        right = self.right_proj(node_feats) # (L, hidden)
        # Outer product: (L, L, hidden*hidden)
        opm = {{b1}}
        return self.opm_output(opm)

    def triangular_update(self, pair_feats):
        """Triangular multiplicative update (outgoing edges).
        pair_feats: (L, L, pair_dim)
        """
        left = self.tri_left(pair_feats) * torch.sigmoid(self.tri_gate_left(pair_feats))
        right = self.tri_right(pair_feats) * torch.sigmoid(self.tri_gate_right(pair_feats))
        # Triangular product: sum over shared index k
        # For outgoing: pair_ij = sum_k left_ik * right_jk
        tri_out = {{b2}}
        return self.tri_output(tri_out)

    def forward(self, node_feats, pair_feats):
        # Outer product mean update
        pair_feats = pair_feats + self.outer_product_mean(node_feats)
        # Triangular multiplicative update
        pair_feats = self.layer_norm(pair_feats)
        pair_feats = {{b3}}
        return pair_feats

questions.title: Potential-Guided Diffusion for Constrained Design
questions.description: Implement potential-guided reverse diffusion that steers protein backbone generation toward desired properties by adding gradient-based guidance to the denoising process.
questions.info: Potential-guided diffusion (Dhariwal & Nichol, NeurIPS 2021) modifies the reverse diffusion process by adding gradients from external potential functions to the denoising score. In protein design, RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) uses potentials to guide generation toward desired properties: binder contacts, symmetry, secondary structure content, and radius of gyration. The guided score is: score_guided = score_unconditional + gamma * grad_x(log p(y|x_t)), where gamma is the guidance scale and p(y|x_t) is the potential evaluated on noisy structures. Multiple potentials can be combined additively for multi-objective design. Chroma (Ingraham et al., Nature 2023) generalizes this with programmatic conditioners. Key considerations include gradient clipping to prevent instability and annealing the guidance strength across timesteps. Reference: Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023); Dhariwal & Nichol, NeurIPS 2021.
questions.code:
import torch

class GuidedDiffusionSampler:
    """Reverse diffusion with potential-based guidance."""
    def __init__(self, denoiser, potentials, guidance_scales, noise_schedule):
        self.denoiser = denoiser
        self.potentials = potentials      # list of potential functions
        self.scales = guidance_scales      # list of floats
        self.schedule = noise_schedule

    def compute_guidance_gradient(self, x_t, t):
        """Compute combined gradient from all guidance potentials."""
        x_t_grad = x_t.detach().requires_grad_(True)
        total_potential = 0.0
        for potential, scale in zip(self.potentials, self.scales):
            total_potential = total_potential + scale * potential(x_t_grad, t)
        total_potential.backward()
        grad = {{b1}}
        return grad

    def guided_reverse_step(self, x_t, t, grad_clip=1.0):
        """One guided reverse diffusion step."""
        alpha_bar_t = self.schedule['alpha_bar'][t]
        beta_t = self.schedule['betas'][t]
        alpha_t = 1 - beta_t
        # Get unguided denoiser prediction
        with torch.no_grad():
            eps_pred = self.denoiser(x_t, t)
        # Compute guidance gradient
        guidance_grad = self.compute_guidance_gradient(x_t, t)
        # Clip gradient for stability
        guidance_grad = torch.clamp(guidance_grad, -grad_clip, grad_clip)
        # Modify predicted noise with guidance
        guided_eps = {{b2}}
        # Compute posterior mean
        x_mean = (1 / torch.sqrt(alpha_t)) * (
            x_t - (beta_t / torch.sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t)) * guided_eps
        )
        # Add noise (except at t=0)
        if t > 0:
            noise = torch.randn_like(x_t)
            x_prev = {{b3}}
        else:
            x_prev = x_mean
        return x_prev

Modify the noise prediction by subtracting the scaled guidance gradient: eps - sqrt(1-alpha_bar)*grad.
Add sqrt(beta_t) scaled noise to the posterior mean for stochastic sampling.

questions.title: Radius of Gyration Potential for Compact Designs
questions.description: Implement a radius of gyration potential used to guide protein diffusion models toward generating compact, globular structures.
questions.info: The radius of gyration (Rg) measures the spatial compactness of a protein structure, defined as the root-mean-square distance of all atoms from the center of mass. Natural globular proteins follow an empirical scaling law: Rg ~ 2.2 * N^0.38, where N is the number of residues (Dima & Thirumalai, J. Phys. Chem. B 2004). In diffusion-based protein design, an Rg potential can be used to guide generation toward compact structures and prevent the generation of extended or unfolded conformations. RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) uses radius of gyration as one of the guidance potentials during reverse diffusion. The Rg potential penalizes structures that are too extended (Rg > expected) or too compact (Rg < expected), steering generation toward the natural compactness. This is particularly important for larger proteins where diffusion models may produce loosely packed structures. Reference: Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023); Dima & Thirumalai, J. Phys. Chem. B 108, 6564-6570 (2004).
