questions.title: Temperature-Stratified Sampling for Sequence Design
questions.description: Implement temperature-stratified sampling for inverse folding, where different regions of the protein (core, surface, interface) use different sampling temperatures to balance conservation and diversity.
questions.info: Temperature-stratified sampling is a practical technique for protein sequence design where different structural regions are sampled at different temperatures. Core residues (buried, hydrophobic) benefit from low temperatures (T~0.1) for conservative, stable designs, while surface residues can use higher temperatures (T~0.3-0.5) for diversity without compromising stability. ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al., Science 2022) supports per-position temperature specification. Interface residues in binder design often use intermediate temperatures to balance binding affinity with diversity. Solvent-accessible surface area (SASA) is commonly used to classify residues as core (SASA < 25%), boundary (25-50%), or surface (>50%). This approach was used in experimental campaigns where diversifying surface residues while conserving core residues increased the success rate of designed proteins. Reference: Dauparas et al., Science 378, 49-56 (2022).

questions.title: Contact Map Prediction Loss for Structure Generation
questions.description: Implement a contact map-based auxiliary loss for protein structure generation, enforcing that the predicted contact map from generated coordinates matches a target contact pattern.
questions.info: Contact maps represent pairwise residue contacts (typically C-beta distance < 8 Angstroms) and serve as a coarse structural descriptor. trRosetta (Yang et al., PNAS 2020) pioneered using predicted inter-residue distances and contacts to guide protein structure generation. In diffusion-based design, a contact map loss can enforce desired tertiary contacts during generation. The loss compares the differentiable contact map computed from generated coordinates with a target contact map using binary cross-entropy. This approach is particularly useful for enforcing long-range contacts that define the protein fold, such as beta-sheet hydrogen bonding patterns and helix packing interactions. Contact-guided generation was a key stepping stone from structure prediction to structure generation. Norn et al. (PNAS 2021) used trRosetta contact predictions as the optimization objective for de novo protein hallucination. Reference: Yang et al., PNAS 117, 1496-1503 (2020); Norn et al., PNAS 118, e2017228118 (2021).

questions.title: Sequence Perplexity for Design Quality Assessment
questions.description: Implement sequence perplexity computation for evaluating the quality of designed protein sequences using a protein language model.
questions.info: Sequence perplexity measures how well a designed protein sequence conforms to the distribution learned by a protein language model (pLM). Low perplexity indicates the sequence is protein-like and evolutionarily plausible. ESM-2 (Lin et al., Science 2023) provides pseudo-perplexity via masked language modeling: each position is masked and the model's predicted probability of the true amino acid is recorded. The geometric mean of inverse probabilities gives perplexity: PPL = exp(-1/L * sum(log p(aa_i))). Designed sequences with perplexity similar to natural proteins (PPL ~10-15 for ESM-2) are more likely to fold and function correctly. ProteinMPNN-designed sequences typically achieve low perplexity because the model captures natural sequence statistics. Very low perplexity may indicate the model is reproducing natural sequences rather than exploring novel designs. Reference: Lin et al., Science 379, 1123-1130 (2023); Dauparas et al., Science 378, 49-56 (2022).

questions.title: DDIM Sampling for Accelerated Protein Generation
questions.description: Implement DDIM (Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models) sampling for faster protein backbone generation with fewer reverse diffusion steps.
questions.info: DDIM sampling (Song et al., ICLR 2021) enables deterministic or near-deterministic reverse diffusion with far fewer steps than standard DDPM, typically 50-100 steps instead of 1000. For protein generation, DDIM is used by RFdiffusion (Watson et al., Nature 2023) and FrameDiff (Yim et al., ICLR 2024) to accelerate inference. DDIM redefines the reverse process as x_{t-1} = sqrt(alpha_bar_{t-1}) * x0_pred + sqrt(1-alpha_bar_{t-1}-sigma^2) * eps_pred + sigma * noise, where sigma=0 gives deterministic sampling. The key insight is that this non-Markovian reverse process produces the same marginal distributions as DDPM but allows step skipping. Timestep subsequences (e.g., every 10th step) are selected, reducing the number of neural network evaluations. DDIM also enables interpolation in latent space between generated structures by controlling the noise trajectory. Reference: Song et al., 'Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models', ICLR 2021; Watson et al., Nature 620, 1089-1100 (2023).
questions.code:
import torch

def ddim_sample_step(denoiser, x_t, t_curr, t_prev, alpha_bar, eta=0.0):
    """One DDIM sampling step for protein backbone generation.
    denoiser: model predicting x0 from (x_t, t)
    x_t: (N, 3) current noisy coordinates
    t_curr, t_prev: current and previous timestep indices
    alpha_bar: precomputed cumulative alpha schedule
    eta: stochasticity parameter (0 = deterministic DDIM)
    """
    alpha_bar_t = alpha_bar[t_curr]
    alpha_bar_prev = alpha_bar[t_prev] if t_prev >= 0 else torch.tensor(1.0)
    # Predict clean structure
    x0_pred = denoiser(x_t, torch.tensor([t_curr]))
    # Compute predicted noise from x_t and x0_pred
    eps_pred = {{b1}}
    # DDIM variance
    sigma = eta * torch.sqrt((1 - alpha_bar_prev) / (1 - alpha_bar_t)) * \
            torch.sqrt(1 - alpha_bar_t / alpha_bar_prev)
    # Direction pointing to x_t
    dir_xt = {{b2}}
    # Compute x_{t-1}
    noise = torch.randn_like(x_t) if eta > 0 and t_prev >= 0 else torch.zeros_like(x_t)
    x_prev = {{b3}}
    return x_prev

def ddim_full_sample(denoiser, num_residues, alpha_bar, num_steps=50, eta=0.0):
    """Full DDIM sampling with step skipping."""
    T = len(alpha_bar)
    # Select timestep subsequence
    timesteps = torch.linspace(T - 1, 0, num_steps + 1).long()
    x = torch.randn(num_residues, 3)
    for i in range(num_steps):
        x = ddim_sample_step(denoiser, x, timesteps[i], timesteps[i + 1], alpha_bar, eta)
    return x
